result.lens: Interview-realism / ordering of clarifying questions vs. requirements at the senior (Amazon SDE-3 / Google L5+) system-design bar
result.orderingVerdict: HYBRID, leaning CLARIFY-FIRST-BUT-FAST. In a real senior loop the strong-hire move is a tight 20-40 second scoping volley FIRST — 2-4 high-leverage clarifiers each stated as a decisive assumption the interviewer can veto ("I'll assume reads dominate writes ~100:1 and we're single-region to start — stop me if not"), and THEN requirements written against that locked scope. You genuinely cannot write correct functional requirements without knowing scope (do we support DMs? group chat? media?), so pure requirements-first risks designing the wrong system; but the junior failure mode is the opposite — a passive 10-question laundry list that hands scoping back to the interviewer. The senior signal is decisiveness UNDER clarification: ask the few questions whose answers fork the architecture, bundle the rest as stated assumptions, and move. So neither the prompt's current pure requirements-first nor a pure clarify-first wins; the right shape is a brief assumption-driven scoping pass merged INTO the front of requirements, not a separate Section 2 that comes after the design-defining requirements are already committed.

findings.area: Section ordering (§1 vs §2) — the central question
findings.problem: The prompt forces requirements (§1, functional + non-functional) to be fully stated BEFORE clarifying questions (§2). This is backwards from real senior practice and is internally incoherent: §1 demands the candidate commit to EXACTLY the core flows that §6 will build ('Whatever you list here is EXACTLY what §6 builds'), yet §2 — which can fork the whole design ('If stock is unlimited I can skip the whole reservation logic') — only runs afterward. A YES/NO answer in §2 can invalidate a functional requirement already locked in §1. At the senior bar you scope first, then commit requirements against that scope.
findings.suggestedChange: Merge a SHORT scoping volley into the FRONT of §1, before the functional-requirements list. Concretely: after the 🎬 structure pitch, add a 2-4 item 'Scope lock' micro-step — the few clarifiers whose answers actually fork the architecture, each phrased as a decisive assumption the interviewer can override ('I'll assume X unless you tell me otherwise'). THEN write functional + non-functional requirements against the locked scope. Demote the current §2 to a residual 'Remaining assumptions' note for the non-forking questions, OR rename §1 to 'Scope & Requirements' and fold §2's forks in. Do not leave design-defining clarifiers stranded after the requirements they would invalidate.

findings.area: Clarifying-question volume & framing (§2)
findings.problem: §2 says 'For EACH question you'd actually ask in a real interview' and gives a heavyweight 4-part template (question + why + design fork + assumption) with no cap. This encourages a long laundry list — the exact junior tell that loses points. A senior asks 2-4 architecture-forking questions in ~30 seconds, not an exhaustive interrogation. Spending an interrogation's worth of airtime before drawing anything reads as stalling/over-process, not seniority.
findings.suggestedChange: Cap clarifiers explicitly: 'Ask only the 2-4 questions whose answer CHANGES THE ARCHITECTURE — if a question doesn't fork the design, make it a stated assumption instead, do not ask it.' Add a guardrail line: 'A senior states decisive assumptions and asks few sharp questions; a long question list reads as junior. Bundle non-forking items as one breath of assumptions.'

findings.area: Latency contract contradicts the company lens (Amazon SDE-3)
findings.problem: <live_latency> (lines 38-46) hard-bans ALL tools and ALL capacity math until after the full opener (FR + NFR + clarifiers are emitted). But the target is Amazon SDE-3, and <company_lens> says Amazon grades on COST and OPERATIONS as first-class and 'work backwards from the customer.' Working backwards from the customer is itself a scoping/clarifying act that belongs at the very top — the prompt's rigid 'requirements then questions then defer all numbers' script fights Amazon's own expected opening move. Also, real interviewers often interrupt within the first minute; a monolithic scripted opener leaves no room for the back-and-forth that actually earns senior signal.
findings.suggestedChange: Add an Amazon-specific note to <live_latency>/§1: 'For Amazon, open by working backwards from the customer in one line and name the 1-2 cost/ops constraints that will drive the design, as part of the scope lock.' Explicitly permit the opener to be INTERACTIVE — e.g. 'pause after the scope lock for the interviewer to redirect before you commit requirements' — rather than streaming a fixed monologue end to end.

findings.area: Non-functional requirements before scale section (§1 vs §3)
findings.problem: §1 asks for non-functional targets as concrete numbers ('availability as 9s, p99 latency') stated BEFORE §3 computes any scale/capacity, and §1 is text-only with no tools. So the candidate commits to specific SLO numbers (e.g. '99.99%, p99 < 200ms') before deriving QPS/storage that would justify them. In a real interview, throwing precise nines before any capacity reasoning can sound like recited boilerplate, and an interviewer may push 'why 99.99 and not 99.9?' with no derivation yet available.
findings.suggestedChange: In §1, instruct the candidate to state NFR targets as ROUGH, defensible bands tied to product impact ('high availability, ~3-4 nines because this is on the money path; p99 in the low hundreds of ms for reads') and explicitly DEFER precise numbers to §3 — 'I'll firm these up once I have the scale.' Add a one-line cross-reference so §1 NFRs and §3 derivations are explicitly reconciled rather than independently asserted.

findings.area: Checkpoint placement vs. front-loaded scoping
findings.problem: <checkpoints> puts the first 🤝 Checkpoint only AFTER Requirements (line 135) and §2 ends with a checkpoint (line 322). But the highest-value interviewer hand-off in a real senior interview is the SCOPE checkpoint — confirming what's in/out BEFORE you spend minutes on requirements and a design. Deferring the first checkpoint until after both §1 and §2 means the candidate can build a full requirements set on an unconfirmed scope.
findings.suggestedChange: Add an explicit scope checkpoint at the END of the scope-lock micro-step (top of §1): '🤝 Checkpoint: here is what I am treating as in-scope and out-of-scope — anything you want to add or cut before I commit requirements?' This is the single most senior hand-off and currently has no home.

findings.area: 'Below the line (out of scope)' is downstream of clarification it depends on
findings.problem: §1 requires a 'Below the line (out of scope)' list as part of requirements, but what is in vs out of scope is precisely what §2's clarifying questions resolve. Declaring out-of-scope items before asking the scoping questions risks the candidate unilaterally cutting something the interviewer wanted (a real strong-hire-killer: 'I assumed group chat was out' when it was the point).
findings.suggestedChange: Move the in/out-of-scope determination into the scope-lock step (top of §1) and frame it as interviewer-confirmable, not a unilateral declaration: 'State what you're cutting AS an assumption to be vetoed, e.g. "I'll treat analytics and multi-region as out of scope for now — flag if either is core."' Tie it to the scope checkpoint above.

findings.area: Opener length / time-realism
findings.problem: The full opener now demands: structure pitch + per-FR (feature + 🗣️ plain words + ⚡ why-hard) + per-NFR (target + 🗣️ plain words + 💥 what-breaks) + a 4-part clarifying block per question + gaps note + multiple 🎙️ scripts — all before any diagram or number. In a 45-50 min interview this is several minutes of monologue before the first box is drawn. Real senior interviews reward getting to a high-level sketch fast and earning depth interactively; an over-produced opener risks the 'breadth with no depth' red flag the prompt itself warns about for Google.
findings.suggestedChange: Add a time budget to <live_latency>: 'The entire opener (scope lock + requirements + clarifiers) should be ~3-4 minutes of talk — keep plain-words/why-hard to one short clause each, not a paragraph. Aim to be sketching the high-level design by minute ~5.' Trim the per-item sub-structure to a single line each in the live path.

result.lens: System-design interview framework alignment — comparing the §1–§11 structure of system_design_prompt.md against the Hello Interview Delivery framework, Alex Xu's 4-step (ByteByteGo), with specific focus on the ordering of Requirements / Clarifying Questions / Core Entities / API / High-Level Design / Deep Dives.
result.orderingVerdict: HYBRID (lean CLARIFY-INTO-REQUIREMENTS, reject the standalone §2). Neither canonical framework treats clarifying questions as a section that comes AFTER requirements: Alex Xu's Step 1 is "understand the problem and establish design scope" via clarifying questions FIRST (assumptions documented there), and Hello Interview folds clarifying directly INTO requirements gathering — you ask targeted questions to surface the functional requirements, listing functional first then non-functional. The prompt inverts this by emitting §1 Requirements first and then a separate §2 Clarifying Questions, which reads backwards (you appear to commit to requirements before asking what shapes them) and duplicates structure. The correct senior move, and the one both frameworks endorse, is to ask the 1-2 scope-defining clarifiers up front and let the answers DRIVE the prioritized functional requirements in the same breath — so clarifying should be interleaved into §1, not a downstream §2. The prompt's live-latency constraint (start talking in seconds) actually reinforces this: a real candidate opens by clarifying scope, then states requirements, never the reverse.

findings.area: Ordering of §1 Requirements vs §2 Clarifying Questions
findings.problem: The prompt puts §1 Requirements (Functional+Non-Functional) BEFORE §2 Clarifying Questions & Assumptions. This contradicts BOTH canonical frameworks. Alex Xu Step 1 is explicitly 'understand the problem and establish design scope' via clarifying questions FIRST — you ask before you scope. Hello Interview folds clarifying INTO requirements (you ask targeted questions to DISCOVER the functional requirements). Stating requirements first and then asking clarifiers reads as backwards to a senior interviewer — it looks like the candidate committed to a scope before checking it, and forces them to either restate or contradict §1 once §2's answers land. The live_latency block (lines 42-44) even orders the spoken opener as 'functional requirements ... then clarifying questions + assumptions,' baking the inverted order into the speakable script.
findings.suggestedChange: Reorder so clarifying comes first or is interleaved. Two acceptable options: (A) Swap to '## 1. Clarifying Questions & Scope' then '## 2. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)', matching Xu Step 1. (B, preferred, matches Hello Interview) Merge them: keep a single '## 1. Requirements' section that OPENS with the 2-3 scope-defining clarifying questions (each with its ↔️ design fork) and then derives the prioritized functional + non-functional requirements from the assumed answers. Update the live_latency opener ordering (lines 42-46) to: structure pitch → 1-2 scope clarifiers + assumptions → functional → non-functional, so the spoken sequence matches.

findings.area: §3 Scale & Capacity placement vs frameworks
findings.problem: The prompt places §3 Scale & Capacity BEFORE §4 Core Entities and §5 API. Neither framework does this. Hello Interview makes capacity estimation the OPTIONAL third sub-step of Requirements and explicitly says 'perform calculations only if they will directly influence your design,' then goes Core Entities → API → High-Level Design. Alex Xu puts back-of-envelope math in Step 2 (high-level design), NOT before the API/entities. Putting a full capacity section as its own §3 ahead of entities/API risks the exact anti-pattern both frameworks warn against: spending interview minutes on vanity math before the design exists to justify it. The prompt's own rigor invariant #1 ('compute only the few numbers that actually influence the design') is in tension with giving capacity a prominent standalone §3 slot.
findings.suggestedChange: Either (a) demote capacity to a short sub-step folded into the Requirements section (Hello Interview style), keeping only the 1-2 decision-driving numbers up front and deferring the rest, or (b) keep §3 but add an explicit instruction at its top: 'Compute ONLY the 1-2 numbers that flip a design decision now; defer all other estimation to the point in §6/§8 where a number actually drives the choice — do not produce a full capacity spreadsheet here.' The prompt already gestures at this in line 45 ('say you're deferring full estimation') — make it the section's governing rule, not an aside.

findings.area: §4 Core Entities → §5 API → §6 High-Level Design ordering
findings.problem: This part of the spine (Core Entities, then API, then High-Level Design built one functional requirement at a time) MATCHES Hello Interview's framework almost exactly (Core Entities ~2min → API ~5min → High-Level Design ~10-15min, going through endpoints/requirements sequentially). This is a strength and should be preserved, not changed. The only minor risk is that the prompt does not surface the time-budget proportions Hello Interview attaches (entities are a quick 2-min foundation, not a deep section), so a candidate could over-invest in §4.
findings.suggestedChange: Keep this ordering as-is — it is correctly aligned. Optionally add a one-line altitude cue to §4 reinforcing what line 332 already hints ('first draft, full fields in the data model'): e.g. 'Keep §4 fast and shallow — it is a 1-2 minute scaffold, not a data model; resist defining fields here.' No structural change needed.

findings.area: §7 Data Model placed AFTER §6 High-Level Design
findings.problem: The prompt separates lightweight Core Entities (§4) from a full Data Model & Storage section (§7) placed after the High-Level Design. Hello Interview's framework has NO separate data-model section — schema columns are documented inline during the High-Level Design step ('document relevant database columns/fields' within HLD). Alex Xu likewise has no standalone data-model step. Splitting entities (§4) from data model (§7) is a reasonable deviation (it lets entities stay fast and defers schema rigor), but it creates a risk the frameworks avoid: the storage-engine choice (the §7 decision cards) is made AFTER the high-level design in §6 already drew the datastores, so §6 must pick a store before §7 justifies it — a potential forward-reference/contradiction.
findings.suggestedChange: This deviation is defensible (it deepens storage rigor beyond what HI/Xu specify) — keep it, but add a cross-reference rule: in §6 instruct 'when you draw a datastore, name the store type but defer the full why/alternatives to §7 — do not re-litigate the choice in both places,' and in §7 open with 'this formalizes the stores introduced in §6; do not introduce a new store here that §6 did not draw.' This preserves the extra rigor while preventing the §6/§7 ordering from producing contradictory or duplicated store justifications.

findings.area: §8 Deep Dives + §9-§11 tail vs frameworks
findings.problem: §8 Deep Dives (Bad→Good→Great, driven by non-functional requirements and bottlenecks) matches both frameworks' deep-dive step well, and the Bad→Good→Great escalation is a genuine IMPROVEMENT over the canonical frameworks (neither HI nor Xu prescribes a tiered progression — it is a strong senior-signal addition). §9 Reliability/Cost, §10 Trade-off Ledger, and §11 Likely Questions collectively map to Alex Xu's Step 4 'Wrap Up' (bottlenecks, error cases, operational issues, recap) but are far more elaborate. The only risk is length: HI/Xu budget ~3-5 min for wrap-up, whereas §9-§11 are three heavy sections — in a live 45-min interview this tail cannot all be spoken.
findings.suggestedChange: Keep §8's Bad→Good→Great — it is a justified improvement; do not revert toward the flatter canonical deep-dive. For §9-§11, add a framing note that these map to the 'wrap-up' phase and are primarily a REFERENCE the candidate draws from when the interviewer probes, not a linear monologue to recite — e.g. at the top of §9: 'In a live interview these three sections are pull-not-push: surface the 2-3 items the interviewer asks about; the full content is your prepared backup.' This reconciles the prompt's thoroughness with the frameworks' short wrap-up budget.

findings.area: Functional-before-non-functional within Requirements
findings.problem: Within §1 the prompt correctly lists Functional requirements first ('above the line'), then Non-Functional — this MATCHES Hello Interview exactly (functional first, then non-functional, prioritize top 3, rest below the line). This is a strength. However, because §2 Clarifying Questions is split out separately, the prompt loses Hello Interview's core mechanic: that you DISCOVER the functional requirements THROUGH targeted clarifying questions. As written, §1 asks the model to assert requirements with no questioning mechanism, then §2 asks questions in a vacuum — the causal link (questions → requirements) that both frameworks rely on is broken.
findings.suggestedChange: Preserve functional-before-non-functional ordering. Restore the questions→requirements causal link by instructing in §1: 'Derive the functional requirements from the answers to your scope clarifiers — show the link (e.g. "Since we assume X from the clarifier above, the core flows are...").' If §2 is kept separate per the HIGH finding above, at minimum have §1 reference the assumptions and §2 reference back, so the two sections are not independent lists.

result.lens: Distributed-systems architect evaluating COMPLETENESS of the HLD interview-coach prompt (system_design_prompt.md, §1-§11 + 12 rigor invariants) and its session builder (hld_session_builder.md). Verdict: the prompt is unusually strong on requirements framing, capacity-derivation rigor, idempotency, single-coordinator analysis, multi-region write topology, RPO/RTO, and trade-off ledgers — it already beats most published rubrics. The gaps are a specific, namable set of senior/staff concerns that are either absent or only implied: back-pressure/load-shedding, observability-as-a-design-section (tracing/metrics/SLO burn-rate is mentioned only once in invariant 11), API versioning + pagination, rate-limiting/quotas as a first-class topic, data lifecycle/TTL/archival, schema-migration mechanics, CAP/PACELC framing, and authz-model/PII depth. Separately, the builder file is materially broken (stale path, divergent weaker checklist) and will degrade output. Findings are concrete prompt edits, prioritized by interview impact at the SDE-3/staff bar.
result.orderingVerdict: CLARIFY-FIRST (with a hybrid nuance). The prompt's current order — §1 Requirements (functional + non-functional) THEN §2 Clarifying Questions — is backwards relative to real senior practice, where strong candidates ask 2-4 scoping questions (scale, read/write ratio, consistency needs, single vs multi-region) BEFORE committing to NFR targets, because the clarifications are what let you defend specific numbers. That said, the prompt's own <live_latency> block already prescribes the correct LIVE order (frame -> functional -> non-functional -> clarifying), so this is really a HYBRID in disguise: a one-line problem frame and the functional-flow list legitimately come first (you can't ask good questions without naming what the system does), but non-functional TARGETS should be stated as provisional and explicitly gated on the clarifying answers. Recommendation: keep functional requirements first, but move clarifying questions ahead of (or interleaved with) the non-functional numeric targets, and have each NFR cite the clarifying answer that justifies its number — so the candidate never states 99.99%/p99=200ms before confirming scale and SLO scope.

findings.area: Builder integrity (hld_session_builder.md) — stale path + divergent checklist
findings.problem: The builder tells the agent to read the production prompt from /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/system_design_prompt.md and to write conversation JSON to /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/data/conversations/. That path does not exist — the real files are under /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/. Worse, the builder embeds its OWN per-section spec (lines 54-135) that silently CONTRADICTS the production prompt: it labels §4 as 'Data Model' and §7 as 'Data Model & Storage' (the production prompt §4 is Core Entities, §5 is API, §6 is High-Level Design), it asks for a 3-part storage card in §7 (production prompt mandates a 5-part card), it omits the §3 talkable-number-and-flip-threshold discipline, and it drops the §6 'Bad->Good->Great' is actually §8 — the section numbering in the checklist is misaligned with the prompt it claims to mirror. An agent following the builder's checklist will produce a structurally different, weaker artifact than the prompt specifies.
findings.suggestedChange: Fix the path to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md (and the data/conversations path) in lines 24-27, 143, 192-235, 242. Then DELETE the builder's redundant §1-§11 mini-spec (lines 52-135) and the quality checklist's section-by-section duplications (lines 263-279), replacing them with a single instruction: 'The section structure, headings, and per-section requirements are defined EXCLUSIVELY by system_design_prompt.md <output_format>. Do not paraphrase or re-derive them here — read that file and follow §1-§11 verbatim.' This removes the contradiction and the maintenance drift.

findings.area: Back-pressure & load shedding (missing as a first-class concern)
findings.problem: Nowhere in the 12 rigor invariants or §8/§9 is back-pressure, load shedding, or admission control named. The prompt covers queues (Kafka) and circuit breakers (builder §9.3 only), but not what happens when ingest outpaces consumers, when a queue fills, or how the system sheds non-critical load to protect the money path. At SDE-3/staff this is a standard probe ('your queue is backing up — now what?') and its absence means candidates will describe unbounded queues that silently grow until OOM.
findings.suggestedChange: Add to rigor invariant 11 (or a new invariant): 'BACK-PRESSURE & LOAD SHEDDING — for the hottest async path, state the bounded-queue behavior (block vs drop vs spill), the admission-control / load-shedding policy under overload (shed read traffic / non-critical writes first, protect the money path), and the signal that triggers it (queue depth, consumer lag, p99 breach).' Also add a one-line prompt to §8 deep-dive coverage list: 'overload behavior: what gets shed, in what order, and how the client learns to back off (429 + Retry-After).'

findings.area: Rate limiting / quotas / abuse (under-specified)
findings.problem: Abuse defense appears only as four words in invariant 11 ('+ abuse defense'). There is no requirement to design a rate limiter (algorithm: token bucket vs sliding window), per-tenant quotas, the shared distributed counter for limits (a global coordinator that invariant 9 would otherwise demand analysis of!), or the client contract (429, Retry-After, quota headers). For an SDE-3 loop this is frequently its own deep dive, and the rate-limiter's distributed-state problem is exactly the kind of single-coordinator analysis the prompt already prizes elsewhere.
findings.suggestedChange: Add a §8 deep-dive coverage bullet: 'rate limiting / quotas — the algorithm (token bucket vs sliding-window log vs fixed-window counter) and WHY, where the limiter state lives (per-node vs shared Redis), how it stays correct under multi-node/multi-region (the shared-counter coordinator — analyze its ceiling per invariant 9), and the client contract (429 + Retry-After + X-RateLimit-Remaining).' Cross-reference invariant 9 so the limiter's counter gets coordinator-level scrutiny.

findings.area: Observability / tracing / metrics (not a design section)
findings.problem: Observability appears only as a clause in invariant 11 ('SLO burn-rate alerts + tracing + runbooks') and is grouped under Amazon-ops emphasis. There is no §9 sub-section requiring the candidate to name the four golden signals / RED-USE method, the trace-propagation story (how a request id flows through gateway -> services -> queue -> worker so a single user action is traceable across the async boundary), the metrics that back each SLO, or what alarms fire and at what threshold. Distributed tracing across the async hop is a classic staff-level differentiator and is currently invisible.
findings.suggestedChange: Add §9E (or fold into §9A): 'Observability — name the key metrics per SLO (the golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, saturation), the burn-rate alert thresholds (e.g. 2% budget in 1h = page), and the END-TO-END TRACING story: how a correlation/trace id is propagated through gateway, services, AND the async queue/worker so one user action is reconstructable across the async boundary. Plain-words gloss for trace id and burn-rate alert.' Promote tracing from a four-word mention to a required, diagrammed concern on the async path.

findings.area: API versioning & pagination (missing in §5)
findings.problem: §5 covers method/path/fields/idempotency and security well, but never requires the candidate to state an API versioning strategy (URI /v1 vs header-based, and the deprecation/compat story) or pagination for any list/feed endpoint (cursor vs offset, and why cursor for large/changing datasets). Every feed, search, and history endpoint needs pagination; offset pagination breaks under concurrent inserts and is a known interview trap. The Microsoft company-lens line even calls for 'a clean, versioned, extensible API' but §5 has no hook to actually produce one.
findings.suggestedChange: Add two bullets to §5 per-endpoint guidance: (1) 'VERSIONING — state the versioning scheme once (e.g. /v1 prefix) and how an additive change vs a breaking change is handled (deprecation window).' (2) 'PAGINATION — for any list/feed/search endpoint, specify cursor-based pagination (opaque cursor token) over offset, and say WHY: offset re-counts and skips/duplicates rows when items are inserted mid-scroll. Show the cursor in the example response.'

findings.area: Data lifecycle / TTL / archival / tiering (absent in §7)
findings.problem: §7 covers store choice, sharding, and consistency thoroughly but says nothing about data lifecycle: TTL/expiry for ephemeral data, hot/warm/cold tiering, archival to object storage, and how the largest table (events/analytics — which invariant 3 forces you to size!) is aged out. Without this, the storage footprint computed in invariant 3 grows unbounded and the candidate has no answer to 'this events table is 50TB/month — then what?' This is the natural companion to the storage-footprint rigor that already exists.
findings.suggestedChange: Add §7 PART D: 'DATA LIFECYCLE — for the largest/fastest-growing table (usually events/analytics), state the retention policy (hot in primary for N days, then archive to S3/object storage / roll into an OLAP warehouse), the TTL/partition-drop mechanism (time-partitioned tables dropped by partition, not row-by-row DELETE), and what is queryable in each tier. Tie the retention window back to the storage $ figure from §3 so the footprint is bounded.'

findings.area: Schema migration / evolution mechanics (under-specified)
findings.problem: Invariant 11 says 'deploy/rollback + reversible migrations' as a clause, but there is no requirement to describe HOW: the expand/contract (parallel-change) pattern, backfilling a new column on a large table without locking, the dual-write/dual-read window, or schema evolution for the event log (Avro/Protobuf compatibility). Migrations on a sharded, live, multi-region system are a staff-level topic and the four words 'reversible migrations' do not force any real reasoning.
findings.suggestedChange: Expand invariant 11's migration clause into its own line: 'SCHEMA EVOLUTION — describe a backward-compatible migration via expand/contract (add nullable column -> dual-write -> backfill in batches -> switch reads -> drop old), how you backfill a large/sharded table without long locks, and for the event log the serialization-compatibility rule (additive-only Avro/Protobuf fields). Name the rollback step for each phase.' Add a §11 extensibility-domain question: 'how do you add a non-nullable column to a 1B-row sharded table with zero downtime?'

findings.area: CAP / PACELC framing (named nowhere)
findings.problem: Invariant 6 demands per-operation consistency and read-your-own-writes (excellent and more useful than a glib label), but the prompt never gives the candidate the vocabulary to FRAME the choice when an interviewer asks the textbook question. There is no mention of CAP or PACELC, so a candidate who has done deep per-operation analysis may still fumble 'so are you CP or AP?' — and the more sophisticated PACELC framing (else-latency-vs-consistency in the no-partition case) is exactly what separates staff candidates. §10's consistency-vs-availability ledger entry is the natural home and currently has no framing hook.
findings.suggestedChange: Add to §10's consistency-vs-availability decision card (and gloss in jargon_guard): 'Frame the consistency stance in CAP/PACELC terms in one plain sentence — e.g. -during a partition we choose CP for the money path (refuse writes rather than diverge) and AP for the feed; and EVEN WITHOUT a partition (the -ELC- of PACELC) we trade latency for consistency on writes by reading from primary.- Then map it to the per-operation table from §7.' Add the gloss: 'PACELC — extends CAP: if Partitioned choose Availability or Consistency; Else (normal operation) choose Latency or Consistency.'

findings.area: Security depth — authz model & PII (thin)
findings.problem: Security is handled as authn + don't-trust-client-input (good for price-tampering) and 'PII/retention/right-to-erasure' as a clause in invariant 11. But there is no requirement to specify the AUTHORIZATION MODEL (RBAC vs ABAC vs resource-ownership checks — 'can user A read user B's order?'), the multi-tenant isolation boundary, encryption-at-rest/in-transit + key management, or a concrete data-classification of which fields are PII and how right-to-erasure is actually executed across replicas/caches/event-log/backups (deletion in an append-only log is a genuinely hard problem the prompt waves at). For SDE-3, authz-on-every-endpoint beyond authn is table stakes.
findings.suggestedChange: Expand the §5/invariant-11 security note: '(1) AUTHZ MODEL — state RBAC vs resource-ownership and show one object-level check (can caller access THIS resource, not just -is logged in-); (2) PII — classify which fields are PII, state encryption at rest + in transit + key rotation, and (3) explain HOW right-to-erasure propagates to replicas, caches, search index, AND the append-only event log (crypto-shredding: delete the per-user encryption key so logged ciphertext becomes unrecoverable).' Add a §11 security-domain question on object-level authz and one on right-to-erasure in an event-sourced log.

findings.area: Capacity-estimation rigor — peak/burst factor & connection limits (incomplete)
findings.problem: §3 + invariants 1-3 are strong on average QPS derivation, cache-miss sizing, and storage footprint. But the rigor does not force a PEAK-TO-AVERAGE / burst multiplier (real systems are provisioned for peak, e.g. 3-10x average, or a thundering-herd flash sale), and it does not require deriving connection-count / connection-pool limits from QPS (the §8 example even cites pool exhaustion, but §3 never sizes the pool). A design sized only on average QPS under-provisions; the flip-threshold discipline should explicitly include the peak case.
findings.suggestedChange: Add to invariant 1/§3: 'Always derive a PEAK number from the average using a stated peak-to-average factor (and name the worst case — flash sale / thundering herd / regional failover doubling load on the survivor). Provision and choose the binding bottleneck from PEAK, not average.' Add one §3 row type: 'Connection budget — derive concurrent connections from QPS x avg latency (Little-s Law: concurrency = QPS x latency_sec) and compare to the DB connection-pool ceiling.'

findings.area: Clarify-vs-requirements ordering (§1 before §2 is backwards for NFR targets)
findings.problem: The output_format puts §1 Requirements (functional AND non-functional, WITH numeric targets like availability-as-9s and p99) BEFORE §2 Clarifying Questions. But the non-functional TARGETS depend on the clarifying answers (scale, region scope, consistency needs). As written, the candidate commits to 99.99%/p99 numbers in §1 and only THEN asks the questions that would justify them — which reads as numbers-pulled-from-air, the opposite of senior signal. The <live_latency> block already prescribes the correct live order (frame -> functional -> non-functional -> clarifying), so §1/§2 as printed also conflicts with the prompt's own latency guidance.
findings.suggestedChange: Either (a) reorder so clarifying questions precede the non-functional numeric targets, or (b) keep §1 first but require each NFR target to be stated as PROVISIONAL and cite the clarifying answer that backs it: add to §1 NFR guidance 'state each numeric target as provisional and tag it with the clarifying answer it depends on (e.g. -p99 200ms ASSUMING single-region read; I confirm region scope in §2-).' Add one line reconciling §1/§2 with <live_latency> so the printed section order matches the spoken order.

findings.area: Idempotency depth — dedup-store sizing, TTL expiry race, and read idempotency
findings.problem: Invariant 7 is strong (natural key + dedup store + TTL + collision proof + define retry return). Two depth gaps remain: (1) it never sizes the dedup store or addresses what happens when a retry arrives AFTER the idempotency-key TTL expires (the retry then re-executes — a real correctness hole the prompt's own TTL emphasis invites), and (2) it frames idempotency only for mutations, but at-least-once delivery also requires idempotent CONSUMERS on the async path (invariant 10 mentions exactly-once but doesn't link it back to the consumer dedup mechanism).
findings.suggestedChange: Add to invariant 7: 'state the dedup-store retention vs the maximum client retry window (TTL must exceed the longest retry/replay horizon or a late retry re-executes — name this race and the bound), size the dedup store from write-QPS x TTL, and ensure the ASYNC CONSUMER is idempotent too (dedup by event id) so at-least-once delivery does not double-apply — link this to invariant 10s delivery guarantee.'

findings.area: Cost-modeling rigor (single line, no unit-economics)
findings.problem: §9D asks for top-3 cost drivers, a rough monthly order-of-magnitude, and the biggest optimization — good directionally, but there is no requirement to compute a UNIT economic ($ per 1M requests, or $ per user/month) which is the figure that actually drives build-vs-buy and is the Amazon-lens favorite. There is also no prompt to compare managed-service vs self-hosted cost, or to tie cost to the peak provisioning from §3 (you pay for peak capacity, not average).
findings.suggestedChange: Add to §9D: 'Compute one UNIT-economics number ($ per 1M requests OR $ per active user / month) from the §3 numbers, not just a total. Note that cost tracks PEAK provisioning (from the peak factor in §3), and name one managed-vs-self-hosted cost trade-off. Tie the dominant driver (often egress/CDN or OLAP) back to the storage footprint from invariant 3.'

findings.area: Multi-region write topology (PACELC/conflict-resolution depth)
findings.problem: Invariant 10 is genuinely strong on multi-region (single-write vs multi-master, cross-region latency, replica lag, delivery guarantee). The one depth gap: for the multi-master branch it does not require a CONFLICT-RESOLUTION mechanism (LWW vs CRDT vs version-vector) or the data-residency/sovereignty constraint (GDPR-style 'EU user data stays in EU') that often FORCES a topology independent of latency. These are the two follow-ups a staff interviewer asks immediately after 'multi-master.'
findings.suggestedChange: Add to invariant 10: 'if multi-master, name the conflict-resolution strategy (last-writer-wins with caveats, CRDT, or version vectors) and what a lost update looks like; and state any data-residency/sovereignty constraint (e.g. region-pinned user data) that may FORCE single-write-region regardless of latency.'

result.lens: Empirical read of two freshly-generated HLD outputs (Flash Sale, Web Crawler), judging whether the prompt's fixed Section-1-Requirements-then-Section-2-Clarifying-Questions ordering reads naturally in practice, plus repetition, deep-dive strength, and capacity-number quality.
result.orderingVerdict: HYBRID, leaning REQUIREMENTS-FIRST but the questions need re-scoping. Empirically the Section-1-before-Section-2 ordering does NOT read badly in these two outputs, and that matches strong senior practice: a candidate states a crisp set of working assumptions/requirements and THEN surfaces the forks, rather than interrogating the interviewer cold. The real defect the outputs reveal is not the order but content overlap: the Section 2 questions are pure scoping questions whose answers were ALREADY baked into Section 1 as hard facts, so Section 2 reads as retroactive justification rather than a genuine pre-commit step (Flash Q2 asks "thousands, or a million?" after Section 1 already assumed 1M; Crawler Q4 asks whether politeness is strict after NFR1 already declared "Politeness is sacred"). The fix is to keep Section 1 first but make its assumptions explicitly PROVISIONAL and let Section 2 own the decision-forks, not to reorder.

findings.area: Section 1 / Section 2 redundancy: questions duplicate already-committed requirements
findings.problem: In BOTH outputs the Section 2 clarifying questions ask things Section 1 already committed to as hard facts, so Section 2 reads as justification-after-the-fact, not scoping-before-commit. Flash Sale: the opener and NFR2 assume '1,000,000 concurrent buyers' and 'K (1,000)', then Q2 asks 'Roughly how many concurrent users hit at the open - thousands, or a million?' and Q1 asks 'Is the inventory truly scarce and fixed at K' - both already answered above. Crawler: NFR1 declares 'Politeness is sacred' and NFR2 fixes '~12K pages/sec / ~1B pages/day', then Q4 asks 'How strict is politeness' and Q2 asks 'target corpus size and how fresh' - already stated as targets. An interviewer hearing requirements stated as settled, then heard re-asked as open questions, notices the seam.
findings.suggestedChange: In the Section 1 output_format spec, require requirements that depend on an unconfirmed answer to be marked provisional inline (e.g. 'assuming ~1M concurrent - confirming in Section 2'). And in the Section 2 spec add a hard rule: 'Do NOT re-ask any quantity or constraint you already asserted in Section 1. A clarifying question must open a fork whose BOTH branches are still live in your mind; if Section 1 already picked the branch, cut the question or convert it to a one-line stated assumption.' Add an instruction to cross-check Section 2 against Section 1 and drop duplicates.

findings.area: Ordering framing: Section 1 commits before Section 2 'asks'
findings.problem: The prompt forces Requirements (Section 1) fully ahead of Clarifying Questions (Section 2) AND tells Section 1 to state talkable NFR targets as numbers. The result in both transcripts is that the genuinely scoping questions (scale, freshness, politeness strictness, JS rendering) - which in a real interview a senior would raise FIRST to shape the design - arrive AFTER the design constraints they should gate. It reads coherently because each Section 2 item carries an 'Assumption I'll proceed with' line, but the logical dependency runs backwards: the design tension in Section 1 ('1M people want 1,000 units') presumes answers Section 2 is still nominally eliciting.
findings.suggestedChange: Keep Section 1 first (it is the stronger interview-control move), but add to the Section 1 spec a short 'Working assumptions (1 line each, will confirm in Section 2)' stub listing the 2-3 load-bearing scope picks, and reframe Section 2's purpose in the prompt from 'questions you'd ask' to 'the forks behind those working assumptions - what changes if I'm wrong.' This makes the order read as 'here's my read, here's where I could be wrong' instead of 'here are facts, now here are questions about those facts.'

findings.area: Cross-section repetition of the same talking points / numbers
findings.problem: Each output restates the one headline number and the core mechanism many times, which bloats the answer. Flash Sale repeats '3M/s vs 100K/s = 30x', '300x cut to 10K/s', and 'sell out in ~100ms' in Section 3 table, Section 3 script, Section 6.2, Section 6.3, the deep-dive math, Section 10, and Section 11 - roughly 6-8 restatements each. The 'atomic Lua decrement, two racers cannot both win' line appears in entities, API, 6.3, deep dive, ledger, and Q-and-A. Crawler repeats '12K fetches/s vs 1/s/domain = 12K domains in parallel' in the opener, Section 3, the ONE-number block, Section 6 final script, the frontier deep dive, the ledger, Section 11, and the 60-second summary. The rigor invariant says feature 'the ONE number' - but the templated per-section Script/one-liner/say-it blocks multiply it well past memorable into repetitive.
findings.suggestedChange: Add a de-duplication directive to output_format: 'The headline number and the single core mechanism should be stated in full ONCE (Section 3 and the deep dive), and thereafter REFERENCED in <=6 words, not re-derived. Section scripts must add NEW information or a new angle, never restate a prior section's number with the same framing.' Consider making the per-section 'one-liner to say out loud' optional when it would merely echo an earlier one-liner.

findings.area: Weakest deep dive: Flash Sale read-your-own-writes is a single-tier stub
findings.problem: The prompt's Section 8 promises Bad to Good to Great escalation as 'where the interview is won', but the 4th Flash Sale deep dive 'How does the buyer reliably see whether they won' is a single Great tier with no Bad/Good, no diagram, no failure matrix, and no decision-forcing math - it is three short paragraphs. Next to the other three richly-tiered dives it reads as a thin add-on, and read-your-own-writes was already covered in the Section 7 consistency table, so it largely repeats. The prompt does allow skipping tiers ('Tiers are OPTIONAL'), but the result here is a dive that does not earn its header.
findings.suggestedChange: Tighten the Section 8 spec: 'Every deep-dive HEADER must justify its weight - if a problem collapses to a single tier with no failure matrix or math, fold it into a related dive or into the consistency table instead of giving it its own "### How do we..." header.' This prevents thin single-tier dives that duplicate Section 7.

findings.area: Capacity numbers are sound; hand-waving risk is only at the cost figures
findings.problem: Verified empirically: every load-bearing capacity figure is arithmetically correct and internally consistent (crawler 30B/30d=11,574 rounded to ~12K, 12K*60=720K rounded ~700K, 100B*9.6bits=120GB, 30B*25KB*3=2.25PB~2.3PB, 8Gbps; flash 1M*3=3M, 3M/100K=30x, 1M/2s=500K, 500K*0.001=500/s). The ONLY soft numbers are the dollar costs, which are asserted without a shown derivation: crawler 'proxy/IP egress ~$1.2M/mo' and 'object storage ~$46K/mo at ~$20/TB-month', and flash 'CDN egress ~2,400 GB per sale (~$120)' and 'burst compute ~$3K-8K'. These violate the rigor demand to COMPUTE numeric estimates by running Python rather than hand-waving - the $1.2M proxy figure in particular has no visible math and drives the 'most expensive part' answer in Section 11.
findings.suggestedChange: In the Section 9D cost spec, add: 'Cost figures are numeric estimates and MUST be computed in capacity.py with the unit math shown (GB/mo x $/GB), not asserted. Show the derivation for the #1 cost driver inline (e.g. 2.5M GB/mo x $0.X/GB = ~$1.2M/mo), since it drives the most-expensive-part answer.' This closes the only real hand-waving gap in otherwise rigorous capacity work.

result.orderingRationale: All 4 reviewers land on HYBRID, not pure clarify-first — so I push back on the prompt owner's "clarify-first" instinct as stated, while agreeing with its core grievance. The honest synthesis: the owner is RIGHT that the current order is wrong (a separate §2 Clarifying Questions sitting AFTER §1 Requirements is backwards and reads as committing-then-questioning), but a literal swap to "§1 Clarifying Questions, §2 Requirements" is also wrong and no reviewer recommends it. The decisive evidence cuts both ways: (a) you genuinely cannot write correct FUNCTIONAL requirements without first naming what the system does and locking the 2-4 forks that change the architecture (do we support group chat? is stock scarce?) — so a scoping pass must come first; but (b) Reviewer 4's empirical read of two real outputs (Flash Sale, Web Crawler) shows the §1-before-§2 order does NOT read badly in practice — the actual defect is CONTENT overlap (§2 re-asks quantities §1 already asserted as hard facts: "thousands or a million?" after §1 already assumed 1M), not sequence. And the canonical frameworks (Reviewer 3: Alex Xu Step 1 = "establish design scope via clarifying questions first"; Hello Interview = clarifying folded INTO requirements discovery) both REJECT clarifying-as-a-downstream-section but ALSO reject a cold interrogation. The senior signal (Reviewer 1) is decisiveness under clarification: ask the few forking questions as vetoes-able assumptions, then commit. The convergent fix is therefore neither swap nor keep — it is MERGE: a short scope-lock at the front of §1, functional requirements derived from it, NON-functional targets stated as provisional and gated on the forks, and the old §2 demoted to "the forks behind my assumptions / what changes if I'm wrong" with a hard no-duplication rule. This also fixes a self-contradiction the reviewers caught: §1 already declares "Whatever you list here is EXACTLY what §6 builds," yet §2 admits a YES/NO answer can "skip the whole reservation logic" — i.e. §2 can invalidate the §1 commitment the prompt treats as locked.

topRecommendations.title: Restructure §1+§2 into a scope-lock-first MERGE (the central ordering fix)
topRecommendations.why: All 4 reviewers flag the §1-before-§2 order as the #1 issue. The current shape forces the candidate to commit functional requirements AND numeric NFR targets (99.99%, p99=200ms) before asking the questions that justify them, and §1's own 'this is EXACTLY what §6 builds' commitment is contradicted by §2's admission that an answer can delete entire flows. Empirically (Reviewer 4) §2 ends up re-asking quantities §1 already asserted, so it reads as retroactive justification, not scoping.
topRecommendations.concreteEdit: Rename §1 to '## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions'. Open it with a NEW 'Scope lock' micro-step: the 2-4 questions whose answer FORKS the architecture, each phrased as a vetoable assumption ('I'll assume reads dominate ~100:1 and single-region to start — stop me if not'), ending with a scope checkpoint ('here's what I'm treating as in/out of scope — add or cut before I commit?'). THEN functional requirements derived from those assumptions (show the link: 'since we assume scarce stock, the core flows are...'). THEN non-functional targets stated as PROVISIONAL bands tagged to the assumption they depend on ('p99 ~200ms ASSUMING single-region read'). Demote the old §2 to a short residual section reframed from 'questions you'd ask' to 'the forks behind my assumptions — what changes if I'm wrong', and add a HARD rule: 'Do NOT re-ask any quantity or constraint already asserted above; if §1 picked the branch, cut the question.' Update the <live_latency> opener ordering (lines 41-44) and <structure_pitch> to match: frame -> scope lock + assumptions -> functional -> provisional non-functional.

topRecommendations.title: Fix the broken session builder (hld_session_builder.md): stale path + contradictory mini-spec
topRecommendations.why: Verified: the builder points at /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/ which DOES NOT EXIST (real files are under /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/, where data/conversations/ confirmed exists). Worse, the builder embeds its OWN §1-§11 spec that contradicts the production prompt — it labels §4 'Data Model' (prod = Core Entities), §6 'High-Level Architecture' (prod = High-Level Design), §7 mandates a 3-part storage card (prod mandates 5-part), and the §8 tier structure diverges. An agent following the builder produces a structurally weaker, mislabeled artifact than the prompt specifies. This is a correctness bug, not a style nit.
topRecommendations.concreteEdit: In hld_session_builder.md: replace all /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/ occurrences (lines 24-27, 143, 192-235, 242) with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/. DELETE the redundant §1-§11 mini-spec (lines 52-135) and the section-by-section duplications in the quality checklist (lines 263-279), replacing both with a single instruction: 'Section structure, headings, and per-section requirements are defined EXCLUSIVELY by system_design_prompt.md <output_format>; read that file and follow §1-§11 verbatim — do not paraphrase or re-derive them here.'

topRecommendations.title: Cap and reframe clarifying questions: 2-4 architecture-forking only, no laundry list
topRecommendations.why: Reviewer 1 (interview-realism) and the builder both push toward volume: §2's heavyweight 4-part template has no cap, and the builder literally says '6-10 questions per category'. A 10-question interrogation before drawing anything is the junior tell that loses points; a senior asks the few questions that fork the design and bundles the rest as stated assumptions. This compounds the ordering fix — capping is what makes the merged scope-lock fast and decisive.
topRecommendations.concreteEdit: In the scope-lock step add: 'Ask ONLY the 2-4 questions whose answer CHANGES THE ARCHITECTURE — if a question does not fork the design, make it a one-line stated assumption instead, do not ask it. A long question list reads as junior; a senior states decisive assumptions and asks few sharp questions.' In the builder, delete '6-10 questions per category' and replace with the same 2-4-forking-questions cap so it stops contradicting the prompt.

topRecommendations.title: Add a global time/altitude budget so the opener does not become a multi-minute monologue
topRecommendations.why: Reviewers 1 and 3 both flag length. The full opener now demands structure pitch + per-FR (feature + plain words + why-hard) + per-NFR (target + plain words + what-breaks) + 4-part clarifying block + gaps + multiple scripts — several minutes of talk before the first box is drawn, risking the 'breadth, no depth' red flag the prompt itself warns about. Reviewer 4 separately shows the headline number gets restated 6-8 times across sections, bloating output. Frameworks budget ~3-5 min for the whole opener and wrap-up.
topRecommendations.concreteEdit: Add to <live_latency>: 'The entire opener (scope lock + requirements) should be ~3-4 minutes of talk — one short clause per plain-words/why-hard line, not a paragraph; aim to be sketching the high-level design by ~minute 5.' Add a de-duplication rule to <output_format>: 'State the headline number and the single core mechanism in FULL once (§3 and its deep dive); thereafter reference in <=6 words, never re-derive. Per-section scripts must add a NEW angle, not restate a prior section's number.' Add a framing note atop §9: 'Sections 9-11 are pull-not-push — surface the 2-3 items the interviewer probes; the rest is prepared backup, not a monologue.'

topRecommendations.title: Add the missing senior/staff design concerns as first-class hooks
topRecommendations.why: Reviewer 2 (distributed-systems architect) identifies a specific, namable set of gaps the otherwise-strong rigor invariants miss — each a standard SDE-3/staff probe: back-pressure/load-shedding (queues are unbounded today), rate-limiting/quotas (only 4 words in invariant 11, and its shared counter is exactly the coordinator invariant 9 prizes), observability/end-to-end tracing across the async hop, API versioning + cursor pagination (§5 has no hook despite the Microsoft lens calling for versioned APIs), and data lifecycle/TTL/archival (the largest table from invariant 3 grows unbounded with no answer to 'this is 50TB/mo — then what?').
topRecommendations.concreteEdit: Add to rigor invariant 11 a back-pressure line ('for the hottest async path, state bounded-queue behavior block/drop/spill, the load-shedding order under overload — shed reads/non-critical writes first, protect the money path — and the trigger signal: queue depth / consumer lag / p99 breach'). Add §8 deep-dive coverage bullets for rate-limiting (algorithm + where limiter state lives + 429/Retry-After client contract, cross-ref invariant 9) and overload behavior. Add §9 observability sub-section (golden signals, burn-rate alert thresholds, and trace-id propagation through gateway->services->queue->worker). Add two §5 bullets: VERSIONING (state /v1 + additive-vs-breaking handling) and PAGINATION (cursor over offset + why). Add §7 PART D: DATA LIFECYCLE (retention window tied back to the §3 storage $ figure, partition-drop not row DELETE).

minorRecommendations: §3 capacity placement: it sits before §4 Core Entities / §5 API; neither canonical framework does this. Don't reorder — add a governing rule at §3's top: 'Compute ONLY the 1-2 numbers that flip a design decision now; defer the rest to the point in §6/§8 where a number drives a choice — no full capacity spreadsheet here' (Reviewer 3).

Add a peak-to-average / burst multiplier to invariant 1/§3 ('always derive a PEAK number from average using a stated factor; provision and pick the binding bottleneck from PEAK, name the worst case — flash sale / regional failover'), and one row deriving connection budget via Little's Law (concurrency = QPS x latency_sec) vs the pool ceiling — §8 cites pool exhaustion but §3 never sizes it (Reviewer 2).

Add CAP/PACELC framing to §10's consistency card + a one-line gloss in jargon_guard, so a candidate who did deep per-operation analysis can still answer the textbook 'are you CP or AP?' cleanly (Reviewer 2).

Deepen idempotency invariant 7: state dedup-store TTL must exceed the longest client retry window (name the late-retry-re-executes race), size the dedup store from write-QPS x TTL, and require the async CONSUMER to be idempotent too (link to invariant 10's delivery guarantee) (Reviewer 2).

Expand security (§5 / invariant 11): require an AUTHZ model (object-level 'can A read B's order?', not just authn), and how right-to-erasure propagates to replicas/caches/search index AND the append-only event log (crypto-shredding) (Reviewer 2).

Multi-region invariant 10: for the multi-master branch require a conflict-resolution mechanism (LWW / CRDT / version vectors) and any data-residency constraint that FORCES single-write-region regardless of latency (Reviewer 2).

Schema migration: expand invariant 11's 'reversible migrations' clause into expand/contract mechanics (add nullable -> dual-write -> batched backfill -> switch reads -> drop), with a §11 question on adding a non-nullable column to a 1B-row sharded table with zero downtime (Reviewer 2).

§9D cost rigor: require ONE unit-economics number ($ per 1M requests or $/user/mo) computed in capacity.py with the unit math SHOWN — Reviewer 4 found the only hand-waving in otherwise-rigorous capacity work was asserted dollar figures (e.g. crawler '$1.2M/mo proxy egress' with no visible derivation, yet it drives the 'most expensive part' answer).

Add a §6/§7 cross-reference rule to prevent the store choice being made in §6 then re-litigated in §7: '§6 names the store type but defers the full why/alternatives to §7; §7 formalizes only stores §6 drew, introduces none new' (Reviewer 3).

Tighten §8 so thin single-tier deep dives don't earn their own header: 'if a problem collapses to one tier with no failure matrix or math, fold it into a related dive or the §7 consistency table' — Reviewer 4 found the Flash Sale read-your-own-writes dive was a 3-paragraph stub duplicating §7.
Add an altitude cue to §4: 'keep this a 1-2 minute scaffold, not a data model — resist defining fields here' (Reviewer 3 — otherwise correctly aligned, leave ordering as-is).

Amazon-lens note for <live_latency>/scope-lock: 'open by working backwards from the customer in one line and name the 1-2 cost/ops constraints that will drive the design' — the current rigid script fights Amazon's own expected opening move; also permit the opener to be interactive (pause after scope lock) rather than a fixed monologue (Reviewer 1).
result.overallAssessment: This prompt is already strong — well above most published rubrics. Its requirements framing, the 12 correct-by-construction rigor invariants (capacity derivation, idempotency, single-coordinator analysis, multi-region write topology, RPO/RTO, trade-off ledger), the Bad->Good->Great deep-dive escalation, and the speakable-script/jargon-guard scaffolding are genuine strengths that two reviewers explicitly called improvements over the canonical Hello Interview / Alex Xu frameworks. It does not need a rewrite. But it has one real structural defect and one real bug that warrant focused work, not polish. (1) The §1-Requirements-then-§2-Clarifying-Questions ordering is backwards — all four independent reviewers converged on this, and the prompt even contradicts itself (§1 'this is EXACTLY what §6 builds' vs §2 'an answer can skip the whole reservation logic'). The fix is a MERGE into a scope-lock-first §1, not the literal clarify-first swap the prompt owner is leaning toward, and not a do-nothing keep. (2) The session builder is materially broken: a stale, nonexistent path and an embedded mini-spec that mislabels and contradicts the production sections — verified, and it will silently degrade every generated artifact; fix it first since it's a 10-minute correctness win. Beyond those, the highest-value additions are a global time budget (the opener has crept into a multi-minute monologue) and a small, namable set of senior/staff gaps (back-pressure, rate-limiting, observability/tracing, pagination/versioning, data lifecycle). Net: keep ~90% as-is, fix the two structural problems, add the time discipline and the five gap hooks, and this is an exceptional interview-coach prompt.
